---
title: "Build LinkedIn Carousels with Typst: the `slide` Layout"
description: |
  A LinkedIn carousel is just a multi-page document. This short tutorial shows
  how a tiny Typst `slide` helper turns every page into a full-bleed, padded
  canvas, then how to compile the deck to PDF and preview frames. It ends with a
  ready-to-paste LLM prompt that drafts a similar carousel from scratch.
date: "2026-05-28"
categories:
  - typst
  - design
  - pdf
  - productivity
image: featured.png
image-alt: |
  Wide social card on a pale grey-green background with a slim emerald rail
  down the left edge. A ruled monospace kicker reads "TYPST · TUTORIAL" above
  large display type that spells "Build LinkedIn carousels with Typst", the
  word "Typst" in emerald, then a smaller line "the slide layout". Stacked
  rounded cards form a motif on the right.
number-sections: false
engine: markdown
extensions:
  typst-render:
    output-directory: ./assets/typst-render
    format: svg
    preamble: _preamble.typ
    cache: false
---

![](featured.png){
  .img-featured
  .img-fluid
  fig-align="center"
  fig-alt='{{< meta image-alt >}}'
  width="600px"
}

## Introduction

A carousel on LinkedIn is not a special object.
It is a multi-page document that the feed turns into swipeable cards, and you upload it as a single PDF.
That reframing is liberating: if you can produce a tidy multi-page PDF, you can produce a carousel.

[Typst](https://typst.app) is a fine tool for exactly that.
You describe each page in plain text, compile to PDF in a fraction of a second, and keep the source under version control like any other code.
No design application, no manual export, no nudging boxes by hand.

This post teaches the one pattern that makes the whole thing pleasant: a small `slide` helper that turns every page into a full-bleed, padded canvas.
Every slide shown below is a real Typst compilation, rendered to SVG by the [`quarto-typst-render`](https://github.com/mcanouil/quarto-typst-render) extension, so you see the code and its result side by side.

::: {.callout-note}

## At a glance

- A carousel page is a near-square 4:3, a calm shape that stands out from the usual tall cards.
- Set the page margin to `0cm` so backgrounds reach every edge.
- A `slide` layout stamps a rail, a page index, and the padding onto every card.
- Compile with `typst compile`, then upload the PDF or rasterise it to frames.

:::

## The page is the canvas

Start by locking the page geometry once, at the top of the file.

```typst
#set document(title: "My Carousel", author: "Your Name")
#set text(lang: "en")
#set page(width: 24cm, height: 18cm, margin: 0cm)
```

The 24cm by 18cm size is a near-square 4:3, a deliberate contrast to the tall 4:5 most carousels reach for.
The exact dimensions do not matter, only the ratio does, so any 4:3 pair works.

The important line is `margin: 0cm`.
Carousel slides want colour and imagery that bleed to every edge, and a page margin would fence the content into a smaller box with white gutters.
Removing the margin hands you the full page, and you reintroduce the breathing room yourself, which is what the `slide` helper is for.

## The `slide` layout

A `slide` helper can do more than add padding.
The most useful ones stamp the recurring *chrome* of the deck onto every page, so each card is born consistent.
The layout below paints a full-height accent rail down the left edge, drops a page index in the top-right corner, then lays out your content in the space that is left.

```{.typst}
#let slide(index: none, fill: paper, body) = page(fill: fill, { // <1>
  place(left + top, rect(width: 0.55cm, height: 100%, fill: accent)) // <2>
  if index != none { // <3>
    place(top + right, dx: -1.4cm, dy: 1.4cm,
      text(font: mono, size: 14pt, fill: ink-soft, index))
  }
  block(width: 100%, height: 100%, inset: (left: 2.6cm, right: 1.7cm, y: 1.7cm), body) // <4>
}) // <5>
```

1. Start a fresh page and paint its background with `fill`, so each call is one card in the carousel.
2. Draw the rail: a thin rectangle pinned to the left edge, the full height of the page. With `margin: 0cm` it bleeds to top, bottom, and side.
3. Drop a small page counter like `02 / 07` in the top-right, but only when an `index` is passed; optional chrome stays out of the way until wanted.
4. Reintroduce the padding `margin: 0cm` removed, with a wider left inset so the content clears the rail.
5. `body` is a trailing content argument, so the helper is called as `#slide({ ... })` with the card's content in the braces.

The helper is optional: a bare `#page(...)` would work too.
Defining `slide` once is what keeps the rail, the index, and the padding identical on every card, and every call site short.

The page geometry, a small palette, the fonts, this `slide` layout, and a ruled `kicker` label are the file's setup.
In this post they live in a shared preamble, so each live example below shows only the card it draws.

## Slides, live

A cover card passes an `index`, pins a `kicker` to the top, and centres the headline.
The rail and the page counter come from the layout for free.

```{typst}
//| echo: true
//| output-filename: "cover.svg"
//| classes: "img-fluid w-50 mx-auto d-block rounded-3 border"
//| alt: "Cover card with a green rail down the left edge, a '01 / 07' index top-right, a ruled 'TYPST · CAROUSEL' kicker, and a large two-line headline 'Build decks from text'."
#slide(index: "01 / 07", {
  kicker("Typst · Carousel")
  v(1fr)
  block(width: 18cm, {
    text(font: display, size: 54pt, weight: "bold", fill: ink, "Build decks")
    linebreak()
    text(font: display, size: 54pt, weight: "bold", fill: accent, "from text")
    v(0.4cm)
    text(size: 20pt, fill: ink-soft, "One layout. A rail, an index, and a full-bleed canvas.")
  })
  v(1fr)
})
```

A content card stacks a few numbered rows with a `grid` to keep the numbers and text aligned.

```{typst}
//| echo: true
//| output-filename: "content.svg"
//| classes: "img-fluid w-50 mx-auto d-block rounded-3 border"
//| alt: "Content card with the rail, a '03 / 07' index, the heading 'Three reasons it fits carousels.', and a numbered list of three short benefits."
#slide(index: "03 / 07", {
  kicker("Why Typst")
  v(0.5cm)
  block(width: 18cm, text(font: display, size: 32pt, weight: "bold", fill: ink, "Three reasons it fits carousels."))
  v(0.7cm)

  let row(n, t) = grid(
    columns: (1.3cm, 1fr),
    align: (left + top, left + top),
    text(font: mono, size: 22pt, weight: "bold", fill: accent, str(n)),
    block(width: 17cm, text(size: 18pt, fill: ink, t)),
  )

  stack(
    dir: ttb,
    spacing: 0.7cm,
    row(1, "Plain text in, PDF out. Diff it, version it, regenerate it."),
    row(2, "One layout sets the chrome of every page at once."),
    row(3, "No design tool, no manual export, no pixel pushing."),
  )
})
```

A closing card flips the background with the `fill` parameter, while the rail and index stay put.

```{typst}
//| echo: true
//| output-filename: "cta.svg"
//| classes: "img-fluid w-50 mx-auto d-block rounded-3 border"
//| alt: "Closing card on a dark green-black background with the rail and a '07 / 07' index, reading 'Compile and post.' above the command to compile the carousel to PDF."
#slide(index: "07 / 07", fill: ink, {
  v(1fr)
  kicker("Ship it")
  v(0.5cm)
  text(font: display, size: 46pt, weight: "bold", fill: paper, "Compile and post.")
  v(0.6cm)
  text(font: mono, size: 17pt, fill: rgb("#8fa49d"), "typst compile carousel.typ carousel.pdf")
  v(1fr)
})
```

Each card renders at the true 4:3 proportion, so what you see is what the feed will show.

## Compile the whole deck

Stack your slides in one `carousel.typ` file, then compile it.
Each `#slide(...)` becomes one page, and the PDF is the carousel.

```bash
# Each #slide is one page; the PDF is the carousel
typst compile carousel.typ carousel.pdf

# Optional: rasterise the pages to frames for a GIF preview
pdftoppm -r 120 carousel.pdf frame -png
```

Upload `carousel.pdf` to LinkedIn as a document post, or stitch the PNG frames into an animated GIF if you want a quick scrollable preview.

::: {.callout-tip}

## Design that survives the feed

- Keep body text at 14pt or larger so it stays legible at thumbnail size.
- Aim for five to seven slides: a cover, the content, and a closing call to action.
- Commit to one visual idea per deck, a palette, a type pairing, a single motif.
- Resist copy-pasting the previous carousel, since variety is what makes each one stop the scroll.

:::

## Bonus: an LLM prompt

If you would rather start from a draft, paste the prompt below into your assistant of choice.
It encodes the geometry, the `slide` helper, and the design constraints, so the output compiles with the `typst` CLI and looks the part.

```text
You are designing a LinkedIn carousel as a Typst document. Produce a single
self-contained carousel.typ that compiles with `typst compile`.

Hard requirements:
- Page geometry: #set page(width: 24cm, height: 18cm, margin: 0cm). This is a
  near-square 4:3; keep the ratio.
- Define one slide layout and use it for every card. It must stamp consistent
  chrome, not just padding:
    #let slide(index: none, fill: <default-bg>, body) = page(fill: fill, {
      place(left + top, rect(width: 0.55cm, height: 100%, fill: <accent>))
      if index != none {
        place(top + right, dx: -1.4cm, dy: 1.4cm,
          text(font: <mono>, size: 14pt, fill: <soft-ink>, index))
      }
      block(width: 100%, height: 100%,
        inset: (left: 2.6cm, right: 1.7cm, y: 1.7cm), body)
    })
  Zero page margin lets the rail bleed to every edge; the inset adds the padding
  back and clears the rail on the left.
- Invent a small palette (background, ink, soft ink, one accent) and a font trio
  (display, body, mono). Prefer fonts that render anywhere; Typst's built-in
  `Libertinus Serif` and `DejaVu Sans Mono` need no installation. No purple,
  lavender, violet, fuchsia, or magenta.
- Five to seven cards following the arc: cover, then content, then a closing
  call to action. Pass an `index` like "03 / 07" to each, and use one card with
  `fill: <accent-or-dark>` to invert the look.
- Body text at least 14pt; headlines 30 to 56pt, so everything reads at thumbnail
  size. Use place, grid, stack, and v()/h() for layout.

Then tell me the two commands to compile it to PDF and to rasterise the pages to
PNG frames.

The topic of the carousel is: <describe your announcement here>.
```

The whole approach comes back to one idea: a single `slide` helper turns a blank page into a carousel canvas, and everything else is just content poured into it.
